result.markdown:
--- title: "Universal Differential Equations: Learning Unknown Mechanisms" subtitle: "Combining mechanistic models with machine learning" format: html engine: julia --- # Introduction ## The mystery of the second wave Recall the Tristan da Cunha 1971 influenza outbreak. When we first examined this data, we noticed something puzzling: after the initial outbreak subsided around day 15, cases re-emerged around day 25-35. A standard SIR model with constant transmission produces only a single peak. We discussed possible explanations: - **Behavioral relaxation**: People resumed normal contact patterns after the first wave - **Waning vigilance**: Initial precautions (isolation, reduced gatherings) were abandoned - **Population structure**: Different groups (households, age groups) infected at different times - **Incomplete isolation**: Some infected individuals weren't fully isolated But which explanation is correct? And what transmission pattern would actually produce the observed dynamics? ## From hypothesis to discovery Throughout this course, we've assumed we know the functional form of disease transmission: $$\beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N}$$ **Universal Differential Equations (UDEs)** offer a different approach: instead of proposing mechanisms and testing them, we can **learn** what transmission function is consistent with the data, while respecting biological constraints. Keep the **known** epidemiological structure: ``` dS/dt = -β(t) * S * I / N # ← Learn β(t) from data! dI/dt = +β(t) * S * I / N - γ * I dR/dt = +γ * I ``` Replace the unknown β with a **neural network** that respects constraints: ``` β(t) = NeuralNetwork([I/N, t]) # Must be positive, bounded ``` ## Learning objectives By the end of this session, you will: 1. Use UDEs to **discover** transmission patterns from data 2. Apply appropriate likelihood functions (Poisson) for count data 3. Interpret learned β(t) in terms of possible mechanisms 4. Understand when UDEs are useful vs. when simpler models suffice 5. Appreciate the challenges of learning from sparse data (~60 points) # Setup ::: {#4 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using DifferentialEquations using Distributions using DataFrames using CSV using Plots using Random using LinearAlgebra using DrWatson using Lux # Modern neural network framework using ComponentArrays using Optimization using OptimizationOptimisers using Optimisers # For Adam optimizer Random.seed!(1234) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` TaskLocalRNG() ``` ::: ::: # Load Tristan da Cunha data ::: {#6 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} flu_tdc = CSV.read(datadir("flu_tdc_1971.csv"), DataFrame) scatter( flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, xlabel = "Time (days)", ylabel = "Daily incidence", label = "Observed cases", markersize = 4, color = :red, title = "Tristan da Cunha 1971: Two waves?", legend = :topright ) vspan!([25, 35], alpha=0.2, color=:blue, label="Second wave?") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: Notice the two distinct periods of transmission: - **First wave** (days 4-12): Sharp peak reaching ~47 cases/day - **Trough** (days 13-24): Near-zero transmission - **Second wave** (days 25-35): Smaller resurgence, peak ~12 cases/day **Question**: What transmission pattern β(t) would produce this? Can we learn it from the data? # Baseline: SIR with constant β First, let's establish what a constant transmission rate gives us: ::: {#8 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ Standard SIR model with constant transmission. """ function sir_constant_beta(du, u, p, t) S, I, R = u β, γ = p N = S + I + R du[1] = -β * S * I / N du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I du[3] = γ * I end # Fit by eye (we'd use MCMC in reality, but we'll keep this quick) β_guess = 0.5 γ_guess = 0.4 u0 = [270.0, 3.0, 0.0] tspan = (0.0, Float64(maximum(flu_tdc.time))) times = Float64.(flu_tdc.time) prob_constant = ODEProblem(sir_constant_beta, u0, tspan, [β_guess, γ_guess]) sol_constant = solve(prob_constant, Tsit5(), saveat=times) # Extract incidence S_values = [sol_constant(t)[1] for t in times] incidence_constant = [0.0; -diff(S_values)] # Plot plot(times, incidence_constant, label="Constant β", linewidth=2) scatter!(flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, label="Observed", markersize=4, color=:red, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Cases", title="Baseline: Can we do better?") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: **Question**: The fit isn't perfect. Could a time-varying β(t) do better? # UDE Approach: Learn β(t) with Neural Network ## Step 1: Define the neural network We'll use a **small** network - remember, we only have 60 data points! ::: {#10 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Network architecture: 2 inputs → 8 hidden → 1 output # Total parameters: ~33 (keeps training fast) rng = Random.default_rng() Random.seed!(rng, 1234) # Define network structure - small for fast ForwardDiff autodiff nn_architecture = Chain( Dense(2 => 8, tanh), # Input: [I/N, t/tmax] Dense(8 => 1, softplus) # Output: β > 0 (softplus ensures positivity) ) # Initialize parameters p_nn, st_nn = Lux.setup(rng, nn_architecture) p_nn = ComponentArray(p_nn) # Convert to ComponentArray for optimization println("Neural network parameters: ", length(p_nn)) println("Data points: ", length(flu_tdc.obs)) println("Ratio: ", round(length(flu_tdc.obs) / length(p_nn), digits=2), " data points per parameter") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Neural network parameters: 33 Data points: 59 Ratio: 1.79 data points per parameter ``` ::: ::: **Critical**: Even with ~1.8 data points per parameter, regularization is still important to prevent overfitting! ## Step 2: Define UDE with learned β(t) ::: {#12 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ SIR model where β is learned by neural network. β = NN([I/N, t/tmax]) predicts transmission rate. """ function sir_ude!(du, u, p, t) S, I, R = u N = S + I + R γ = p.γ # Keep recovery rate fixed (known from literature) # Neural network predicts β(t, I) # Inputs: prevalence (I/N) and normalized time (t/tmax) tmax = p.tmax nn_input = [I/N, t/tmax] # Evaluate neural network β_learned, _ = nn_architecture(nn_input, p.nn_params, st_nn) β = β_learned[1] # Extract scalar # Standard SIR dynamics with learned β du[1] = -β * S * I / N du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I du[3] = γ * I end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Main.Notebook.sir_ude! ``` ::: ::: ## Step 3: Prediction and loss function ::: {#14 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ Predict incidence using current UDE parameters. """ function predict_ude(p_all) # Solve ODE with current parameters prob_ude = ODEProblem(sir_ude!, u0, tspan, p_all) sol_ude = solve(prob_ude, Tsit5(), saveat=times, dense=false) # Extract incidence if sol_ude.retcode != :Success return fill(NaN, length(times)) end S_pred = [sol_ude.u[i][1] for i in 1:length(times)] incidence_pred = [0.0; -diff(S_pred)] return incidence_pred end """ Loss function using Poisson negative log-likelihood. Recall from our observation models session: choosing a likelihood is really choosing a distance metric! The Poisson NLL weights residuals differently than SSE: - SSE: All residuals contribute equally → dominated by peak (large counts) - Poisson NLL: Penalizes relative errors → small counts matter more This isn't about Poisson being "correct" - it's a pragmatic choice that forces the model to explain the second wave (where counts are small but nonzero). """ function loss_ude(p_all, _) pred = predict_ude(p_all) # Poisson negative log-likelihood # NLL = sum(λ - y*log(λ)) where λ = predicted, y = observed # Add small ε to avoid log(0) ε = 1e-6 obs = flu_tdc.obs nll = sum(pred .- obs .* log.(pred .+ ε)) # L2 penalty on NN weights l2_penalty = 1e-4 * sum(abs2, p_all.nn_params) return nll + l2_penalty end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Main.Notebook.loss_ude ``` ::: ::: **Why does the loss function matter?** As we learned in the observation models session, different likelihoods are really different distance metrics: - **SSE**: A residual of 10 at the peak (expected ~40) counts the same as at the tail (expected ~2) - **Poisson NLL**: Penalizes predicting 0 when cases occur - forces the model to explain the second wave This isn't about Poisson being "mechanistically correct" (it's not!) - it's about choosing a distance that cares about small counts. ## Step 4: Train the UDE This is where the magic happens. We use **ForwardDiff** for automatic differentiation, which is fast for small parameter counts like our 33-parameter network. ::: {#16 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Combine all parameters p_all = ComponentArray( nn_params = p_nn, γ = γ_guess, tmax = maximum(times) ) println("Starting UDE training...") println("="^60) # Initial loss println("Initial loss: ", round(loss_ude(p_all, nothing), digits=2)) # Use ForwardDiff for fast automatic differentiation opt_func = OptimizationFunction(loss_ude, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff()) opt_prob = OptimizationProblem(opt_func, p_all, nothing) # Adam optimizer - works well for neural network training println("\nTraining with Adam optimizer (100 iterations)...") @time result = solve(opt_prob, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=100, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 20 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("\n" * "="^60) println("Final loss: ", round(result.objective, digits=2)) p_trained = result.u ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Starting UDE training... ============================================================ Initial loss: -24.77 Training with Adam optimizer (100 iterations)... Iter 20: loss = -185.77 Iter 40: loss = -200.88 Iter 60: loss = -209.05 Iter 80: loss = -219.36 Iter 100: loss = -233.33 Iter 100: loss = -233.33 3.930169 seconds (33.57 M allocations: 1.757 GiB, 6.19% gc time, 97.65% compilation time) ============================================================ Final loss: -233.33 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
ComponentVector{Float64}(nn_params = (layer_1 = (weight = [-0.8653867410669673 -0.617209005033249; -0.0976614705882676 0.21740191813901732; … ; -1.1007547482747781 -1.5167361599242004; -1.4274511247949824 2.3684302663402432], bias = [-0.21607351536233613, -0.43638368952279405, 0.3955071642785022, 0.5698130082287703, 0.10663290413348521, 0.21031965913347284, -0.5235550028181978, 0.2571507722868688]), layer_2 = (weight = [-0.12406088660106881 0.003391512255644429 … -0.2929284649063042 -0.4306102951957171], bias = [0.003833345668078999])), γ = 0.26988190276610635, tmax = 58.14993963965447)
``` ::: ::: ::: **Expected behavior**: - Loss should decrease from ~100000 to ~1000-2000 - Training takes only a few seconds with ForwardDiff - The small network (33 parameters) is fast to optimize ## Step 5: Compare results ::: {#18 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Get UDE predictions pred_ude = predict_ude(p_trained) # Plot comparison plot(times, incidence_constant, label="Constant β", linewidth=2, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Cases", title="UDE vs Constant β") plot!(times, pred_ude, label="UDE (learned β)", linewidth=2) scatter!(flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, label="Observed", markersize=4, color=:red) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: **Key question**: Does the UDE capture the second wave? Look at the fit around days 25-35. ## Step 6: Visualize learned β(t) The key output - what transmission pattern did the network learn? ::: {#20 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Extract learned β(t) for representative I values t_plot = range(0.0, maximum(times), length=100) I_representative = 50.0 # Mid-epidemic prevalence β_learned_over_time = [begin input = [I_representative / 273.0, t / maximum(times)] β_val, _ = nn_architecture(input, p_trained.nn_params, st_nn) β_val[1] end for t in t_plot] # Plot with wave markers plot(t_plot, β_learned_over_time, label="Learned β(t)", linewidth=2, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="β", title="Learned Transmission Rate", legend=:topright) hline!([β_guess], label="Constant β baseline", linestyle=:dash) vspan!([25, 35], alpha=0.2, color=:blue, label="Second wave period") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: **Interpreting the learned β(t)**: If β(t) shows a **dip followed by recovery** around days 15-25, this is consistent with: - **Behavioral relaxation**: Initial caution (isolation, reduced gatherings) followed by return to normal behavior - The model "discovered" that transmission must increase again to explain the second wave If β(t) is **roughly constant**, the Poisson likelihood wasn't enough to force learning the second wave pattern - we might need: - More training iterations - Less regularization - A larger network # Practical Challenges ## Why is this hard? 1. **Small dataset** (60 points) vs **many parameters** (33) 2. **Identifiability**: Many β(t) curves could fit the data 3. **Regularization tuning**: Finding the right penalties is an art 4. **Initialization**: Different random seeds → different solutions 5. **ODE stiffness**: Training can be numerically unstable ## Common failure modes ### Overfitting ```julia # If your loss is very low but predictions look crazy → overfitting # Solution: Increase regularization (1e-3 → 1e-2) ``` ### Underfitting ```julia # If UDE ≈ constant β → network learned nothing # Solution: Decrease regularization (1e-3 → 1e-4) # Or increase network capacity ``` ### NaN explosion ```julia # If you get NaN errors → numerical instability # Solution: Reduce learning rate (0.01 → 0.001) # Add gradient clipping # Use more robust ODE solver ``` ## Sensitivity to hyperparameters Let's test if our solution is robust by trying different regularization strengths. ::: {#22 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Helper for Poisson NLL with varying regularization function make_loss(reg_scale) return function(p, _) pred = predict_ude(p) ε = 1e-6 obs = flu_tdc.obs nll = sum(pred .- obs .* log.(pred .+ ε)) l2_pen = (1e-4 * reg_scale) * sum(abs2, p.nn_params) return nll + l2_pen end end # Test different regularization scales for (name, scale) in [("0.5×", 0.5), ("1.0×", 1.0), ("2.0×", 2.0)] println("Testing regularization scale: $name") loss_fn = make_loss(scale) opt_func_test = OptimizationFunction(loss_fn, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff()) opt_prob_test = OptimizationProblem(opt_func_test, p_all, nothing) result_test = solve(opt_prob_test, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=50, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 25 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("Final loss ($name): ", round(result_test.objective, digits=2), "\n") end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing regularization scale: 0.5× Iter 25: loss = -193.0 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Final loss (0.5×): -204.78 Testing regularization scale: 1.0× Iter 25: loss = -192.99 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Final loss (1.0×): -204.78 Testing regularization scale: 2.0× Iter 25: loss = -192.99 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Iter 50: loss = -204.78 Final loss (2.0×): -204.78 ``` ::: ::: **Interpreting sensitivity results**: - If lower regularization gives much better fit → model may need more flexibility to capture the second wave - If results are similar across scales → solution is robust - If results vary wildly → model is unstable (common with 60 points!) # When to use UDEs ## Use UDEs when: ✅ You have **mechanistic knowledge** about structure (compartments) ✅ But **unknown functional forms** (transmission, observation process) ✅ You have **moderate-to-large datasets** (ideally 100s-1000s of points) ✅ You're willing to **experiment** and **tune hyperparameters** ✅ **Exploratory analysis** - discovering unknown mechanisms ## Don't use UDEs when: ❌ Simple parametric models work fine (Occam's razor!) ❌ Very small datasets (<50 points) without strong regularization ❌ You need **interpretable** parameters for publication ❌ Computational resources are limited (training is expensive) ❌ Real-time inference needed (MCMC may be faster per dataset) # Comparison with other approaches | Method | Flexibility | Interpretability | Data needs | Computational cost | |--------|-------------|------------------|------------|-------------------| | **Constant β** | Low | High | Low (10-50 points) | Very low | | **Time-varying β with splines** | Medium | Medium | Medium (50-200) | Low | | **GP for β(t)** | Medium | High | Medium (50-200) | Medium | | **UDE** | High | Low | High (100-1000+) | High | **For TdC data (60 points)**: GPs or splines likely better than UDEs! # Real success stories UDEs work well when: 1. **Large spatiotemporal datasets**: COVID-19 with daily data from 100+ locations 2. **High-frequency measurements**: Lab experiments with measurements every hour 3. **Known structure, unknown details**: Chemical reactions, climate models 4. **Discovery research**: Finding unknown mechanisms (not just fitting) # Exercises 1. **Network size**: Try smaller (4→1) and larger (16→8→1) networks. How does data fit change? Overfitting? 2. **Regularization tuning**: Systematically vary regularization weights. Plot loss surface. Find optimal balance. 3. **Input features**: Try different NN inputs: - Just time: β(t) - Just prevalence: β(I/N) - Both: β(I/N, t) - Add S/N: β(S/N, I/N, t) Which works best? 4. **Synthetic data test**: Generate data with known β(t) = β₀ * (1 + 0.3*sin(2πt/20)). Can UDE recover it? 5. **Ensemble uncertainty**: Train 10 UDEs with different random seeds. Plot ensemble of β(t) curves. How variable are they? # Summary **What we learned about UDEs**: 1. UDEs can **discover** transmission patterns consistent with data while respecting biological constraints 2. The **loss function matters**: Poisson likelihood forces the model to explain all cases, including the second wave 3. Small datasets (60 points) make learning challenging - regularization prevents overfitting but may also prevent discovering complex patterns 4. Learned β(t) can suggest mechanisms (e.g., behavioral relaxation) but doesn't prove them **What we learned about the second wave**: If the UDE learned a β(t) that increases around days 20-25: - This is consistent with **behavioral relaxation** after the first wave - The population may have resumed normal contact patterns - This is **hypothesis generation**, not proof - we'd need independent evidence **Key insight**: UDEs are tools for **discovery under constraints**. They work best when: - You have mechanistic knowledge to constrain the model - You use appropriate likelihoods (Poisson for counts) - You interpret results as hypotheses, not conclusions **For the Tristan da Cunha data**: The two-wave pattern remains partially mysterious. A UDE with Poisson likelihood may suggest behavioral mechanisms, but with 60 points, conclusions are tentative. This is exactly the kind of exploratory analysis UDEs are designed for! # Next steps Now that you've seen modern inference methods (variational inference, UDEs), you understand: - **When to use traditional methods** (MCMC, fixed models) - **When to use fast approximations** (VI, mean-field) - **When to use hybrid approaches** (UDEs, GPs) The best modelers know **all** the tools and choose wisely! # References - Rackauckas et al. (2020). "Universal Differential Equations for Scientific Machine Learning." arXiv:2001.04385 - Dandekar et al. (2020). "Neural ODE vs Conventional ODE in COVID-19 Forecasting." NeurIPS Workshop - Rackauckas et al. (2021). "Generalized Physics-Informed Learning Through Language-Wide Differentiable Programming." - DiffEqFlux.jl documentation: https://docs.sciml.ai/DiffEqFlux/ - SciML tutorials: https://docs.sciml.ai/Overview/stable/showcase/missing_physics/
